config.title: Renal Physiology — High Yield Step 1
config.subtitle: GFR, tubular transport, acid-base, diuretics, Starling forces, and hormonal regulation

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman at 22 weeks gestation presents for a routine prenatal visit. Her blood pressure is 102/64 mmHg, HR 78 bpm. Serum creatinine is 0.5 mg/dL. She reports no urinary symptoms. Her pre-pregnancy creatinine was 0.9 mg/dL. Which of the following best explains the change in her serum creatinine during pregnancy?
choices: Increased renal plasma flow raises GFR, increasing creatinine clearance
Progesterone-mediated tubular secretion of creatinine is upregulated
Decreased muscle mass during pregnancy reduces creatinine production
Placental uptake of creatinine removes it from maternal circulation
Estrogen directly inhibits proximal tubule reabsorption of creatinine
questions.explanation: During pregnancy, renal plasma flow increases by ~50–80% and GFR rises by ~40–60%, driven by vasodilation and increased cardiac output. This higher GFR means creatinine is filtered and cleared more rapidly, reducing serum levels. Creatinine is neither reabsorbed nor significantly secreted under normal conditions, making choices B and E incorrect. Muscle mass is not meaningfully reduced in early-to-mid pregnancy (choice C), and there is no placental uptake of creatinine (choice D). A serum creatinine of 0.9 mg/dL in a pregnant woman is actually abnormal and warrants evaluation.
keypoints: ⭐ GFR increases ~50% in pregnancy — serum Cr and BUN fall; 'normal' values may mask renal disease
⭐ Renal plasma flow rises ~50–80% beginning in the first trimester
Creatinine is freely filtered and minimally secreted — its clearance approximates GFR
Serum Cr > 0.8 mg/dL in pregnancy warrants workup for underlying renal pathology

questions.stem: A researcher is studying glomerular filtration in an isolated kidney preparation. She measures the following: plasma oncotic pressure 28 mmHg, Bowman's space oncotic pressure 0 mmHg, glomerular capillary hydrostatic pressure 60 mmHg, Bowman's space hydrostatic pressure 18 mmHg. She then infuses a drug that selectively constricts the efferent arteriole. Which of the following best describes the immediate effect of efferent arteriolar constriction on the net filtration pressure?
choices: Net filtration pressure increases due to a rise in glomerular capillary hydrostatic pressure
Net filtration pressure decreases due to a fall in glomerular capillary hydrostatic pressure
Net filtration pressure is unchanged because oncotic forces compensate
Net filtration pressure increases due to a fall in Bowman's space hydrostatic pressure
Net filtration pressure decreases due to a rise in plasma oncotic pressure
questions.explanation: Net filtration pressure = (Pgc – PBS) – (πgc – πBS). Efferent arteriolar constriction impedes outflow from the glomerular capillary, raising glomerular capillary hydrostatic pressure (Pgc) and thus increasing the net filtration pressure and GFR — at least acutely. This is the mechanism by which angiotensin II, which constricts the efferent arteriole preferentially, helps maintain GFR when renal perfusion pressure falls. Choice B describes afferent constriction. Choice E is ultimately true if filtration fraction rises markedly (oncotic pressure rises along the capillary), but this is a downstream effect, not the primary driving change.
keypoints: ⭐ Net filtration pressure = (Pgc − PBS) − (πgc − πBS); normal ≈ 14 mmHg
⭐ Efferent constriction → ↑ Pgc → ↑ GFR (acutely); also ↑ filtration fraction
Afferent constriction → ↓ Pgc → ↓ GFR
ACE inhibitors preferentially dilate efferent arteriole → ↓ GFR in bilateral RAS or single functional kidney

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with longstanding type 2 diabetes presents for follow-up. His serum creatinine is 1.2 mg/dL, BUN 18 mg/dL, and urine creatinine is 108 mg/dL. A 24-hour urine collection volume is 1,440 mL. His body weight is 80 kg. Which of the following is the best estimate of his creatinine clearance?
choices: 108 mL/min
90 mL/min
54 mL/min
144 mL/min
72 mL/min
questions.explanation: Creatinine clearance (CrCl) = (Urine Cr × Urine Volume) / (Plasma Cr × Time). Urine flow rate = 1440 mL / 1440 min = 1 mL/min. CrCl = (108 mg/dL × 1 mL/min) / 1.2 mg/dL = 90 mL/min — wait, let me re-examine: (108 × 1) / 1.2 = 90. The correct answer should be 90 mL/min. Actually: (U_Cr × V̇) / P_Cr = (108 × 1) / 1.2 = 90 mL/min. Distractor 108 reflects forgetting to divide by plasma creatinine; 54 reflects halving in error; 144 reflects using a 2-day collection volume mistakenly. A GFR of 90 mL/min/1.73m² places him in CKD stage G2 if persistent.
keypoints: ⭐ CrCl formula: (U_Cr [mg/dL] × V̇ [mL/min]) ÷ P_Cr [mg/dL]
⭐ Cockroft-Gault estimates CrCl: [(140 − age) × weight] / (72 × Cr); ×0.85 for females
CrCl overestimates true GFR because creatinine is also secreted by proximal tubule (~10–20%)
Inulin clearance = gold standard for true GFR (freely filtered, not secreted or reabsorbed)

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman with hypertension is started on a thiazide diuretic. Two weeks later she reports muscle cramps. Labs: Na⁺ 138, K⁺ 2.9, Cl⁻ 96, HCO₃⁻ 30, BUN 14, Cr 0.8 mEq/L. Urine Cl⁻ is 8 mEq/L. Which of the following best explains the elevated bicarbonate in this patient?
choices: Volume contraction raises aldosterone, increasing proximal tubule bicarbonate reabsorption and H⁺ secretion
Thiazides directly inhibit carbonic anhydrase in the proximal tubule, generating excess bicarbonate
Hypokalemia causes intracellular acidosis, driving bicarbonate into cells from plasma
Increased distal Na⁺ delivery stimulates anion exchange of bicarbonate for chloride
Loop segment reabsorption of bicarbonate is upregulated by elevated aldosterone
questions.explanation: Thiazides cause volume depletion by blocking NaCl reabsorption in the distal convoluted tubule. Volume contraction activates the RAAS, and the resulting aldosterone surge drives Na⁺-H⁺ exchange and H⁺-ATPase in the collecting duct, increasing net acid excretion. Simultaneously, contraction alkalosis occurs: a fixed amount of bicarbonate is distributed in a now-smaller ECF volume, raising its concentration. Thiazides do not directly inhibit carbonic anhydrase (choice B — that is acetazolamide). Hypokalemia does cause intracellular acidosis but this drives H⁺ into cells and K⁺ out, worsening alkalosis rather than being its primary cause (choice C is a contributing mechanism, not the primary one). The low urine Cl⁻ (< 10 mEq/L) confirms chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from volume depletion.
keypoints: ⭐ Thiazide → metabolic alkalosis via volume contraction (contraction alkalosis) + hypokalemia
⭐ Urine Cl⁻ < 10 mEq/L = chloride-responsive alkalosis (vomiting, diuretics, post-hypercapnia)
Hypokalemia perpetuates alkalosis: K⁺ exits cells in exchange for H⁺, which then gets excreted
Treatment: IV NaCl (restores volume) + KCl replacement

questions.stem: A 19-year-old male college student presents with periorbital edema and frothy urine. Serum albumin is 1.8 g/dL, total protein 4.2 g/dL. BUN 12 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL. Urinalysis shows 4+ protein and oval fat bodies. Blood pressure is 88/60 mmHg. Which of the following best explains why this patient develops edema despite a low blood pressure?
choices: Loss of plasma oncotic pressure allows fluid to shift from the vascular space into the interstitium
Increased glomerular hydrostatic pressure drives fluid from capillaries into the interstitium
Impaired lymphatic drainage prevents reabsorption of interstitial fluid
Elevated venous hydrostatic pressure from right heart dysfunction increases capillary filtration
Increased capillary permeability from glomerular inflammation allows protein and fluid to leak
questions.explanation: In nephrotic syndrome, massive proteinuria (>3.5 g/day) leads to hypoalbuminemia, which dramatically lowers plasma oncotic pressure. According to Starling forces, the driving force for capillary reabsorption is reduced, and fluid accumulates in the interstitium as edema. The hypotension (not hypertension) and low creatinine make heart failure (choice D) and glomerulonephritis (choice E) unlikely. Lymphatic impairment (choice C) causes localized edema (e.g., lymphedema), not the generalized edema of nephrotic syndrome. Increased glomerular hydrostatic pressure (choice B) affects filtration into Bowman's space, not systemic interstitial edema.
keypoints: ⭐ Nephrotic syndrome: >3.5 g/day proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, edema, hyperlipidemia, lipiduria
⭐ Edema mechanism: ↓ albumin → ↓ πplasma → net outward Starling force → interstitial fluid accumulation
Oval fat bodies (lipid-laden renal tubular cells) on UA = pathognomonic for nephrotic syndrome
Most common cause in adults: focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS); in children: minimal change disease

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with heart failure and CKD stage 3 (GFR 38 mL/min) is admitted for acute decompensation with 3+ pitting edema and pulmonary crackles. He is already taking furosemide 40 mg daily at home without adequate diuresis. Which of the following best explains why loop diuretics may have reduced efficacy in this patient's CKD?
choices: Accumulated organic anions in CKD compete with furosemide for secretion into the tubular lumen
Reduced GFR decreases the filtered load of sodium available for furosemide to block
CKD causes downregulation of the Na⁺-K⁺-2Cl⁻ cotransporter in the thick ascending limb
Uremic toxins directly bind furosemide in the plasma, preventing its distribution to the kidney
CKD increases renal prostaglandin synthesis, which antagonizes furosemide at its receptor
questions.explanation: Loop diuretics like furosemide are weak organic acids that must be secreted into the tubular lumen by OAT1/OAT3 transporters in the proximal tubule to reach their luminal target (NKCC2 in the thick ascending limb). In CKD, retained endogenous organic anions (uremic toxins) accumulate and compete with furosemide for these transporters, reducing tubular secretion and therefore luminal drug concentration. Choice B is incorrect — the filtered load of sodium is actually still substantial even in moderate CKD. Choices C, D, and E describe mechanisms not supported by evidence. The clinical response is to increase the furosemide dose (to overcome the competition) or add a thiazide for synergistic blockade.
keypoints: ⭐ Loop diuretics require proximal tubular secretion (OAT1/3) to reach luminal NKCC2 — CKD impairs this
⭐ Strategy in CKD: ↑ loop diuretic dose or add thiazide (sequential nephron blockade)
Furosemide ceiling dose: 80–160 mg in CKD; bumetanide and torsemide have better oral bioavailability
NKCC2 inhibition also blocks the lumen-positive potential that drives paracellular Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ reabsorption → hypocalcemia, hypomagnesemia

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with a long history of COPD on home oxygen presents with increasing confusion. ABG: pH 7.38, PaCO₂ 58 mmHg, PaO₂ 62 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 34 mEq/L. Serum Na⁺ 140, K⁺ 4.1, Cl⁻ 96. Which of the following renal mechanisms is primarily responsible for maintaining her near-normal pH?
choices: Increased proximal tubular H⁺ secretion and bicarbonate generation in response to elevated PCO₂
Decreased renal excretion of bicarbonate due to a reset osmostat lowering the bicarbonate threshold
Upregulation of distal tubule Cl⁻/HCO₃⁻ exchangers to retain bicarbonate and excrete chloride
Increased ammonia synthesis by proximal tubule cells, buffering urinary H⁺ and raising plasma bicarbonate
Aldosterone-independent Na⁺-H⁺ exchange in the collecting duct that retains bicarbonate
questions.explanation: In chronic respiratory acidosis, elevated PaCO₂ drives CO₂ into renal tubular cells, increasing intracellular carbonic acid. This stimulates Na⁺-H⁺ exchangers and H⁺-ATPases in the proximal tubule, increasing H⁺ secretion into the lumen. The accompanying filtered bicarbonate is fully reabsorbed and new bicarbonate is generated (via titration of urinary buffers and ammoniagenesis), raising plasma HCO₃⁻. This renal compensation is the reason her pH is near-normal despite a PaCO₂ of 58. Choices C and E describe mechanisms that do not account for the primary driver (elevated CO₂ → direct tubular stimulation). Choice B describes osmostat resetting seen in SIADH, not respiratory acidosis. Ammoniagenesis (choice D) contributes but is not the primary initial mechanism.
keypoints: ⭐ Chronic respiratory acidosis: compensation = renal HCO₃⁻ retention; expected HCO₃⁻ rises 3.5 mEq/L per 10 mmHg ↑ PaCO₂
⭐ Acute respiratory acidosis: HCO₃⁻ rises only 1 mEq/L per 10 mmHg ↑ PaCO₂ (buffering only)
Normal pH despite high PaCO₂ = chronic, fully compensated respiratory acidosis
Do NOT correct CO₂ rapidly in chronic hypercapnia — removing drive for respiratory effort may cause apnea

questions.stem: A 33-year-old woman is found to have hypertension and hypokalemia on routine labs. Na⁺ 148, K⁺ 2.6, HCO₃⁻ 32, Cr 0.7 mg/dL. Plasma renin activity is suppressed and aldosterone level is markedly elevated. 24-hour urine aldosterone is elevated. CT abdomen shows a 1.8 cm left adrenal adenoma. Which segment of the nephron is primarily responsible for the potassium wasting in this patient?
choices: Cortical collecting duct principal cells
Proximal convoluted tubule
Thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle
Macula densa cells
Thin descending limb of the loop of Henle
questions.explanation: Aldosterone acts on principal cells of the cortical collecting duct (CCD) by binding cytoplasmic mineralocorticoid receptors, which increase transcription of ENaC (apical Na⁺ channel) and Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase (basolateral). Increased luminal Na⁺ reabsorption creates a lumen-negative potential that drives K⁺ secretion through ROMK channels into the lumen, causing urinary K⁺ wasting. The proximal tubule (choice B) reabsorbs ~65% of filtered K⁺ but is not aldosterone-sensitive. The thick ascending limb (choice C) reabsorbs K⁺ via NKCC2 but does not secrete it. Macula densa cells (choice D) are chemosensors for tubuloglomerular feedback, not transport sites. This clinical picture — hypertension, hypokalemia, suppressed renin, elevated aldosterone, adrenal adenoma — is classic primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn syndrome).
keypoints: ⭐ Aldosterone acts on CCD principal cells → ↑ ENaC + ↑ Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase → Na⁺ retention + K⁺ wasting
⭐ Conn syndrome: HTN + hypokalemia + suppressed renin + elevated aldosterone → adrenal adenoma
Alpha-intercalated cells (also in CCD) secrete H⁺ → explains concurrent metabolic alkalosis
Screening test: aldosterone-to-renin ratio > 30 with aldosterone > 15 ng/dL

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man presents to the ED after ingesting a large amount of aspirin in a suicide attempt 4 hours ago. ABG: pH 7.49, PaCO₂ 28 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 21 mEq/L. Serum Na⁺ 140, K⁺ 3.5, Cl⁻ 105, HCO₃⁻ 21 mEq/L. Which of the following best describes his acid-base disturbance?
choices: Mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis
Pure respiratory alkalosis with appropriate renal compensation
Mixed metabolic alkalosis and respiratory alkalosis
High anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
Normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with respiratory alkalosis
questions.explanation: Classic salicylate toxicity produces a mixed disorder: salicylates directly stimulate the central respiratory center → hyperventilation → respiratory alkalosis (pH 7.49, PaCO₂ 28); simultaneously, salicylate uncouples oxidative phosphorylation and accumulates as an organic acid → high anion gap metabolic acidosis (AG = 140 − 105 − 21 = 14; borderline elevated, expected to worsen). Expected HCO₃⁻ for a pure respiratory alkalosis of PaCO₂ 28 would be: 24 − (1.5 × [40−28]/10) = 24 − 1.8 = 22.2 mEq/L — slightly higher than the observed 21, suggesting a concurrent mild metabolic acidosis pulling HCO₃⁻ down. This mixed picture is pathognomonic for salicylate toxicity. Pure respiratory alkalosis (choice B) would not lower HCO₃⁻ this much acutely.
keypoints: ⭐ Salicylate toxicity = mixed respiratory alkalosis + high anion gap metabolic acidosis — pathognomonic
⭐ Anion gap = Na⁺ − (Cl⁻ + HCO₃⁻); normal = 8–12 mEq/L; correct for albumin (↑ AG by 2.5 per 1 g/dL ↓ albumin)
MUDPILES mnemonic: Methanol, Uremia, DKA, Propylene glycol, Isoniazid/Iron, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol, Salicylates
Treatment: urinary alkalinization with NaHCO₃ → ionizes salicylate → traps it in urine → ↑ excretion

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with cirrhosis and ascites is admitted with acute confusion and oliguria. Urine output is 200 mL over 24 hours. Labs: BUN 52 mg/dL, creatinine 3.4 mg/dL, Na⁺ 128 mEq/L, urine Na⁺ < 10 mEq/L, urine osmolality 520 mOsm/kg. Urine microscopy shows no casts. Volume challenge with normal saline does not improve creatinine. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of renal dysfunction in this patient?
choices: Splanchnic vasodilation reduces effective arterial blood volume, activating renal vasoconstriction via RAAS and sympathetics
Hepatic failure impairs angiotensinogen synthesis, reducing renal perfusion pressure
Portal hypertension directly obstructs renal venous outflow, increasing intraglomerular pressure
Excess aldosterone from the diseased liver causes afferent arteriolar constriction
Hypoalbuminemia reduces glomerular oncotic pressure, impairing tubular reabsorption
questions.explanation: Hepatorenal syndrome (HRS) results from extreme splanchnic vasodilation (driven by portal hypertension, NO, and other vasodilators), which reduces effective arterial blood volume. The body interprets this as hypovolemia, activating the RAAS, sympathetic nervous system, and ADH. The resulting intense renal vasoconstriction — especially of afferent arterioles — dramatically reduces GFR. Crucially, the kidneys themselves are structurally normal (hence no casts, and excellent urine concentrating ability). Choice B is incorrect — cirrhosis often increases angiotensinogen-derived angiotensin II. Choice C confuses venous anatomy. The key diagnostic feature distinguishing HRS from pre-renal azotemia is failure to respond to volume challenge.
keypoints: ⭐ HRS pathophysiology: splanchnic vasodilation → ↓ effective circulating volume → renal vasoconstriction → ↓ GFR
⭐ HRS diagnosis: Cr ≥ 1.5 after 2 days of albumin + diuretic withdrawal, no response to fluids, no other cause
Urine Na⁺ < 10, high urine osmolality, no casts — kidneys are intact but underperfused
Treatment: terlipressin (vasopressin analog) + IV albumin; definitive = liver transplant

questions.stem: A 41-year-old woman with no past medical history is found to have a serum sodium of 128 mEq/L on routine labs. She has no edema, her skin turgor is normal, and BP is 112/74 mmHg. Urine osmolality is 310 mOsm/kg and urine Na⁺ is 55 mEq/L. TSH and morning cortisol are normal. She reports taking a new medication for depression. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of hyponatremia in this patient?
choices: Non-osmotic ADH release reduces free water excretion, diluting plasma sodium
Inhibition of the renin-angiotensin system impairs aldosterone-mediated Na⁺ reabsorption
Increased thirst and water intake overwhelm normal urinary diluting capacity
Direct inhibition of Na⁺-K⁺-ATPase in collecting duct principal cells reduces sodium reabsorption
Osmotic shift of intracellular water into plasma dilutes extracellular sodium
questions.explanation: This patient has euvolemic hyponatremia with an inappropriately concentrated urine (Uosm > 100 mOsm/kg) and elevated urine Na⁺, consistent with SIADH. SSRIs — the most likely new antidepressant — are a well-recognized cause of SIADH via non-osmotic stimulation of ADH (vasopressin) release. Elevated ADH inserts aquaporin-2 channels in the collecting duct, increasing free water reabsorption and diluting serum sodium. The urine remains inappropriately concentrated. Psychogenic polydipsia (choice C) causes dilute urine (Uosm < 100). RAAS inhibition (choice B) would cause hyperkalemia and volume depletion, not euvolemic hyponatremia. Osmotic shifts (choice E) occur with hyperglycemia or mannitol.
keypoints: ⭐ SIADH diagnostic criteria: hyponatremia + Uosm > 100 + U-Na > 40 + euvolemia + normal thyroid/adrenal
⭐ Common SIADH causes: SSRIs, antipsychotics, carbamazepine, pulmonary disease, CNS pathology, malignancy
ADH acts on V2 receptors in collecting duct → PKA → aquaporin-2 insertion → ↑ water reabsorption
Treatment: fluid restriction first; for severe/symptomatic: hypertonic saline; vaptans for chronic SIADH

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man with a history of aortic stenosis develops progressive oliguria after cardiac catheterization. Urine output drops to 150 mL in 12 hours. BUN is 48 mg/dL and creatinine rises from 1.0 to 2.6 mg/dL over 2 days. Urine microscopy shows muddy brown granular casts and renal tubular epithelial cells. Which of the following best explains the mechanism by which this patient maintains reduced urine output despite severe tubular injury?
choices: Tubular debris and cellular casts obstruct luminal flow, reducing filtrate delivery to the collecting duct
Loss of tubular integrity allows filtrate to leak back across damaged epithelium into the interstitium
Afferent arteriolar constriction reduces GFR, decreasing the volume of filtrate entering the tubule
Sloughing of tubular epithelial cells activates macula densa TGF, constricting afferent arterioles
Vasoactive substances released from ischemic tubules dilate efferent arterioles, reducing filtration pressure
questions.explanation: This patient has acute tubular necrosis (ATN) from contrast-induced renal ischemia (a procedural complication). ATN reduces urine output through two complementary mechanisms: (1) tubular obstruction by cellular debris and casts, and (2) back-leak of filtrate through the denuded, leaky tubular epithelium into the peritubular interstitium. Back-leak (choice B) is the mechanism that best explains why, despite continued (if reduced) GFR, urine output is markedly decreased — the filtrate re-enters the interstitium rather than progressing to the collecting system. Tubular obstruction (choice A) also contributes but back-leak is the primary explanation for the discrepancy between GFR and urine output in established ATN. TGF via macula densa (choice D) is a real component of ATN pathophysiology but involves intact TGF signaling, not sloughing per se.
keypoints: ⭐ ATN mechanisms of oliguria: (1) tubular back-leak of filtrate + (2) tubular obstruction by casts
⭐ Muddy brown granular casts + RTECs = pathognomonic for ATN
ATN phases: initiation → maintenance (1–2 weeks, oliguria) → recovery (polyuria as tubules regenerate)
FENa > 2% in ATN (tubules can't reabsorb Na); FENa < 1% in pre-renal azotemia

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with recurrent kidney stones, muscle weakness, and constipation. Serum Ca²⁺ is 11.8 mg/dL, phosphorus 2.1 mg/dL, PTH 145 pg/mL (elevated). Which of the following best describes PTH's primary mechanism of action on the kidney to explain the hypercalcemia?
choices: PTH stimulates distal tubule Ca²⁺ reabsorption via upregulation of TRPV5 channels and activates 1α-hydroxylase to increase calcitriol synthesis
PTH directly increases intestinal Ca²⁺ absorption by binding receptors on enterocytes
PTH inhibits proximal tubular phosphate reabsorption without affecting calcium handling
PTH increases osteoclast-mediated bone resorption independently of renal mechanisms
PTH stimulates proximal tubule Ca²⁺ reabsorption and suppresses 1α-hydroxylase activity
questions.explanation: PTH exerts two major renal effects that elevate serum calcium: (1) it increases distal tubular Ca²⁺ reabsorption via cAMP-mediated upregulation of apical TRPV5 (epithelial Ca²⁺ channel) and basolateral NCX/PMCA transporters; and (2) it activates 1α-hydroxylase in the proximal tubule, converting 25-OH vitamin D to 1,25-(OH)₂D (calcitriol), which then drives intestinal Ca²⁺ absorption. The direct intestinal effect (choice B) is mediated by calcitriol, not PTH itself binding enterocytes. PTH also inhibits phosphate reabsorption (choice C) — this is correct but incomplete as it omits Ca²⁺ reabsorption. Bone resorption (choice D) contributes to hypercalcemia but is not the 'primary renal mechanism.'
keypoints: ⭐ PTH renal effects: (1) ↑ distal Ca²⁺ reabsorption (TRPV5), (2) ↑ 1α-hydroxylase → calcitriol, (3) ↓ proximal phosphate reabsorption
⭐ Primary hyperparathyroidism: ↑ Ca²⁺, ↓ PO₄, ↑ PTH, ↑ urine Ca²⁺, ↑ ALP if bony disease
PTH acts via Gs → adenylyl cyclase → ↑ cAMP in renal tubular and osteoblastic cells
Kidney stones in hyperPTH: hypercalciuria (filtered load exceeds reabsorptive capacity) → calcium oxalate stones

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and a GFR of 55 mL/min/1.73m² is started on an SGLT2 inhibitor. His physician explains that the drug will lower blood glucose and provide renal protection. Which of the following best explains the renoprotective mechanism of SGLT2 inhibitors beyond glucose lowering?
choices: SGLT2 inhibition reduces proximal tubule Na⁺ reabsorption, increasing macula densa Na⁺ delivery and activating tubuloglomerular feedback to reduce afferent arteriolar tone and GFR
SGLT2 inhibition increases urinary glucose excretion, osmotically drawing water into the tubule and reducing glomerular hydrostatic pressure
SGLT2 blockade upregulates GLUT2 transporters in the proximal tubule, redistributing glucose away from glomerular mesangial cells
SGLT2 inhibition reduces tubular oxygen consumption, preventing hypoxic injury to the outer medullary thick ascending limb
SGLT2 inhibitors activate AMPK in podocytes, stabilizing the slit diaphragm and reducing proteinuria directly
questions.explanation: In diabetes, hyperglycemia upregulates SGLT2, causing excess proximal tubule Na⁺ and glucose reabsorption. This depletes NaCl delivery to the macula densa, blunting tubuloglomerular feedback (TGF) and causing pathological afferent arteriolar dilation → glomerular hyperfiltration → progressive nephron loss. SGLT2 inhibitors restore Na⁺ delivery to the macula densa, re-engaging TGF and constricting the afferent arteriole, reducing intraglomerular pressure and GFR — halting the hyperfiltration injury. This mechanism explains their efficacy even in non-diabetic CKD (CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD trials). Choice D is a metabolic benefit but not the primary renoprotective mechanism. Choices B, C, and E describe implausible or secondary mechanisms.
keypoints: ⭐ SGLT2 inhibitor renoprotection: restore macula densa Na⁺ delivery → TGF re-engagement → ↓ afferent tone → ↓ glomerular hyperfiltration
⭐ CREDENCE trial: canagliflozin ↓ renal composite outcomes 30% in diabetic CKD; DAPA-CKD: dapagliflozin effective in non-diabetic CKD
SGLT2 in proximal tubule: reabsorbs ~90% of filtered glucose (cotransported with Na⁺, 2:1 ratio)
Side effects: euglycemic DKA, UTI/genital mycotic infections, Fournier gangrene (rare)

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman is referred for evaluation of recurrent metabolic acidosis. Labs: Na⁺ 138, K⁺ 3.0, Cl⁻ 114, HCO₃⁻ 12 mEq/L. Serum AG = 12. Urine pH is 6.8. Urine anion gap is positive (+12). She has no diarrhea. Which of the following best explains the pathophysiology of her acid-base disturbance?
choices: Defective H⁺-ATPase in alpha-intercalated cells prevents adequate urinary acidification despite systemic acidosis
Defective Na⁺-HCO₃⁻ cotransporter in the proximal tubule reduces bicarbonate reabsorption, wasting it in the urine
Aldosterone deficiency impairs Na⁺ reabsorption in the principal cell, reducing the lumen-negative potential needed for H⁺ secretion
Carbonic anhydrase II deficiency prevents bicarbonate generation in both proximal and distal tubular cells
Excess ammonium production in the proximal tubule overwhelms distal buffering capacity, causing acidosis
questions.explanation: This patient has a normal anion gap (hyperchloremic) metabolic acidosis with a positive urine anion gap (+12), which indicates impaired urinary NH₄⁺ excretion (since UAG ≈ −[NH₄⁺]; a positive UAG means NH₄⁺ is low). A urine pH of 6.8 during systemic acidosis confirms the inability to appropriately acidify the urine — the hallmark of distal (Type 1) RTA. In distal RTA, alpha-intercalated cell H⁺-ATPase dysfunction prevents H⁺ secretion, so neither titratable acid nor NH₄⁺ is generated appropriately, and urine pH cannot drop below 5.3. Proximal RTA (Type 2, choice B) causes bicarbonate wasting but can still acidify urine once serum HCO₃⁻ falls below the reabsorptive threshold. Type 4 RTA (choice C) is associated with hyperkalemia, not hypokalemia.
keypoints: ⭐ Distal RTA (Type 1): urine pH > 5.3 despite acidosis, hypokalemia, positive UAG, nephrocalcinosis/stones
⭐ Urine anion gap = (U-Na + U-K) − U-Cl; negative = appropriate NH₄⁺ excretion (GI cause); positive = renal H⁺ wasting
Type 2 (proximal) RTA: HCO₃⁻ wasting above threshold, urine can acidify, hypokalemia, Fanconi syndrome
Type 4 RTA: hyperkalemia + hypoaldosteronism (diabetes, ACEi) — most common RTA in clinical practice

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with chronic renal failure (GFR 18 mL/min) presents with fatigue and dyspnea on exertion. Hemoglobin is 8.2 g/dL, MCV 88 fL, reticulocyte count 0.4%. Iron studies show normal ferritin and transferrin saturation. Which of the following best explains the primary mechanism of anemia in this patient?
choices: Decreased erythropoietin synthesis by peritubular fibroblasts due to loss of functional renal mass
Uremic inhibition of intestinal iron absorption leading to functional iron deficiency
Shortened red blood cell survival due to uremic toxin-mediated oxidative hemolysis
Folate deficiency from dialysis-related losses impairing erythropoiesis
Bone marrow suppression by retained parathyroid hormone causing aplastic anemia
questions.explanation: The anemia of CKD is primarily normocytic, normochromic, and hypoproliferative — driven by inadequate erythropoietin (EPO) synthesis. EPO is produced by peritubular interstitial fibroblasts in the renal cortex (not tubular cells), and as functioning nephron mass declines, EPO output falls disproportionately. The low reticulocyte count confirms inadequate erythropoietic stimulation, not hemolysis or iron deficiency. Iron deficiency (choice B) can coexist but is not the primary mechanism here given normal iron studies. Uremic hemolysis (choice C) and PTH suppression (choice E) are minor contributing factors. Folate losses (choice D) apply specifically to dialysis patients, not CKD stage 5 pre-dialysis.
keypoints: ⭐ EPO produced by peritubular cortical fibroblasts; stimulated by hypoxia via HIF-1α
⭐ CKD anemia: normocytic, normochromic, low reticulocyte count → treat with ESAs (epoetin alfa) + iron
Target Hgb with ESA therapy: 10–11.5 g/dL; higher targets increase cardiovascular event risk (TREAT trial)
Other CKD anemia contributors: iron deficiency (most treatable), blood loss from dialysis, inflammation (↑ hepcidin)

questions.stem: A physiology student is studying the countercurrent multiplier system. She notes that the descending thin limb of the loop of Henle is highly water-permeable but poorly permeable to NaCl. The ascending thin limb is impermeable to water but permeable to NaCl. The thick ascending limb actively pumps NaCl out of the lumen. Which of the following best explains how this arrangement generates a hypertonic medullary interstitium?
choices: Active NaCl reabsorption in the thick ascending limb creates a local osmotic gradient that draws water from the descending limb, concentrating tubular fluid and driving further NaCl reabsorption downstream
Water secretion into the descending limb by aquaporin-1 channels dilutes the tubular fluid, generating a gradient for NaCl to diffuse out of the ascending limb
Passive urea reabsorption from the collecting duct into the medullary interstitium independently drives the osmotic gradient without requiring NaCl transport
ADH activates NaCl pumps in the descending thin limb, increasing medullary tonicity by driving sodium into the interstitium
The vasa recta actively pump solute into the medullary interstitium, sustaining the concentration gradient independent of tubular flow
questions.explanation: The countercurrent multiplier works as follows: active NaCl reabsorption in the water-impermeable thick ascending limb (TAL) makes the medullary interstitium hypertonic and the ascending tubular fluid hypotonic. This hypertonic interstitium osmotically withdraws water from the water-permeable descending limb, concentrating tubular fluid as it descends. By the tip of the loop, tubular fluid is maximally concentrated. As this concentrated fluid ascends through the NaCl-permeable (but water-impermeable) thin ascending limb, NaCl diffuses out passively, amplifying the medullary gradient. The gradient is sustained, not created, by urea (choice C) — urea recycling from the inner medullary collecting duct adds to interstitial tonicity but is a secondary amplifier. Water is not secreted into the descending limb (choice B); rather, it leaves the lumen osmotically. The vasa recta (choice E) preserve the gradient via countercurrent exchange, not active pumping.
keypoints: ⭐ TAL is the 'single effect' generator: active NKCC2 pumping → hypertonic interstitium → countercurrent multiplication
⭐ Descending limb: aquaporin-1 expressed, water exits → tubular fluid concentrates to ~1200 mOsm at loop tip
Urea recycling from inner medullary collecting duct (UT-A1/UT-A3) adds ~600 mOsm to inner medulla
Vasa recta = countercurrent exchangers (not multipliers): maintain gradient by minimizing solute washout

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with gout and hypertension presents to clinic. He is currently taking hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily. His uric acid is 9.8 mg/dL. His physician considers adding probenecid to lower uric acid. Which of the following best explains the mechanism by which probenecid lowers serum uric acid?
choices: Probenecid inhibits URAT1 transporters in the proximal tubule, blocking urate reabsorption and increasing urinary uric acid excretion
Probenecid inhibits xanthine oxidase, reducing de novo synthesis of uric acid from hypoxanthine
Probenecid blocks OAT1 and OAT3 transporters in the proximal tubule, preventing tubular secretion of urate
Probenecid activates ABCG2 transporters in the intestine, increasing intestinal elimination of uric acid
Probenecid alkalinizes the urine, increasing the solubility of uric acid and reducing renal tubular precipitation
questions.explanation: Uric acid handling in the proximal tubule involves filtered urate being reabsorbed by URAT1 (apical) and OAT4/10 transporters. Probenecid inhibits URAT1, blocking urate reabsorption from the tubular lumen, and thus increases urinary uric acid excretion (uricosuric effect). Xanthine oxidase inhibition (choice B) is the mechanism of allopurinol and febuxostat — not probenecid. OAT1/3 (choice C) mediate tubular secretion of many drugs including penicillin; probenecid does block these but that would reduce secretion (decrease urine urate) — the opposite of its therapeutic intent. Importantly, probenecid should not be used in patients with uric acid overproducers or urolithiasis given the increased urinary urate load.
keypoints: ⭐ Probenecid = uricosuric agent: inhibits URAT1 (proximal tubule urate reabsorption) → ↑ urine uric acid
⭐ Allopurinol/febuxostat = xanthine oxidase inhibitors → ↓ uric acid production (preferred in overproducers)
Thiazides ↑ uric acid: compete with urate for OAT secretion AND cause volume contraction → ↑ reabsorption
Probenecid also blocks tubular secretion of penicillin (historic use to prolong antibiotic levels)

questions.stem: A 77-year-old man with benign prostatic hyperplasia and recurrent UTIs is admitted for sepsis. His creatinine has risen from a baseline of 1.1 to 4.2 mg/dL over 5 days. Renal ultrasound shows bilateral hydronephrosis. Urine output is 100 mL/day. After bladder catheterization, there is immediate drainage of 800 mL of urine. Over the following 48 hours, urine output increases to 4–6 liters/day. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of the post-obstructive polyuria?
choices: Accumulated urea and other osmoles act as an osmotic diuretic while tubular dysfunction impairs concentrating ability
Pressure-mediated downregulation of aquaporin-2 channels persists after obstruction relief, causing nephrogenic DI
Rapid restoration of GFR overwhelms the reabsorptive capacity of structurally damaged tubules
ANP released in response to volume expansion from bladder drainage directly inhibits ADH secretion
Back-pressure atrophy of the medullary interstitium destroys the countercurrent gradient, permanently impairing urine concentration
questions.explanation: Post-obstructive diuresis has two major mechanisms: (1) osmotic diuresis from retained urea and other solutes that were unable to be excreted during obstruction — these act as obligate osmoles pulling water into the tubule; and (2) acquired tubular dysfunction from the obstructive injury, impairing Na⁺ and water reabsorption in the thick ascending limb and collecting duct. AQP2 downregulation (choice B) is a real contributor but is not the primary mechanism. Restored GFR overwhelming damaged tubules (choice C) is partly true but incompletely describes the osmotic diuresis component, which is the dominant early driver. ANP (choice D) plays a minor ancillary role. Permanent medullary gradient loss (choice E) overstates the irreversibility; most renal function recovers after relief of obstruction.
keypoints: ⭐ Post-obstructive diuresis: osmotic diuresis (retained urea/solutes) + tubular dysfunction (↓ AQP2, ↓ NKCC2) → polyuria
⭐ Management: replace ~50% of urine output with 0.45% NaCl to avoid iatrogenic hypernatremia
Do NOT clamp catheter to 'prevent' post-obstructive diuresis — physiologic diuresis is expected and beneficial
Watch for electrolyte losses: hyponatremia, hypokalemia, and hypomagnesemia during high-output phase

questions.stem: A 22-year-old competitive cyclist presents with dark urine after a 100-mile race. He drank water throughout the race. Labs: Na⁺ 128 mEq/L, K⁺ 3.8, Cr 0.9 mg/dL, plasma osmolality 262 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality 88 mOsm/kg. There is no proteinuria or hematuria on dipstick. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of his hyponatremia?
choices: Excess free water intake overwhelms maximal urinary dilution capacity, diluting plasma sodium
Exercise-induced ADH release causes inappropriately concentrated urine and free water retention
Sweat sodium losses are replaced with free water, causing net sodium depletion
Rhabdomyolysis releases intracellular water into plasma, diluting extracellular sodium
Gastrointestinal sodium losses from vomiting reduce total body sodium
questions.explanation: This patient has hypotonic, euvolemic hyponatremia with a maximally dilute urine (Uosm 88 mOsm/kg, near the minimum of ~50–100 mOsm/kg), indicating that ADH is appropriately suppressed and the kidneys are excreting free water. The hyponatremia therefore results from an intake of free water (hypotonic fluid) that exceeds the kidney's maximum excretory capacity — a phenomenon called exercise-associated hyponatremia (EAH) from psychogenic polydipsia in endurance athletes. Contrast this with SIADH (choice B), which would cause inappropriately concentrated urine (Uosm > 100) with elevated urine sodium. The maximally dilute urine is the critical discriminating feature. Dark urine raises concern for myoglobinuria/rhabdomyolysis, but Cr is normal and there is no dipstick blood with negative RBCs.
keypoints: ⭐ Exercise-associated hyponatremia (EAH): dilute urine (Uosm < 100) + hyponatremia = excessive hypotonic fluid intake
⭐ Distinguish from SIADH: EAH has dilute urine (suppressed ADH); SIADH has concentrated urine (elevated ADH)
Minimum urine osmolality = ~50 mOsm/kg (maximum dilution); maximum free water excretion ~15–20 L/day
Treatment of EAH: fluid restriction; severe symptomatic cases: hypertonic saline (same as SIADH)
